They must replant vegetation and reshape the terrain.

column1: Geologists identify rocks by examining their characteristics. Igneous rocks contain interlocking crystals with no layers. Sedimentary rocks show distinct horizontal bands. These layers formed as particles settled over time. Metamorphic rocks display wavy or bent layering. Heat and pressure created these distorted patterns. The presence or absence of layers provides the first clue to rock type.

column1: Simple tests help identify rocks in the field. The scratch test reveals hardness. Soft minerals like talc scratch easily with a fingernail. Hard minerals like quartz scratch glass. The acid test identifies carbonate minerals. Geologists drop dilute acid on limestone. Fizzing bubbles indicate calcite present. Crystal size also matters. Large visible crystals suggest slow underground cooling.

column1: Geologic maps display information about rocks beneath the surface. Contour lines connect points of equal elevation. When lines cluster close together the slope is steep. Widely spaced lines mean gentle terrain. Different colors and patterns represent different rock types. Red might indicate granite while blue shows limestone. Strike and dip symbols show how rock layers tilt underground. Geologists use these maps to predict where valuable minerals might occur.

column1: Field geologists map rock formations by observing outcrops. An outcrop is where bedrock pokes through soil and plants. Geologists record the rock type at each outcrop location. They measure how layers tilt using a compass and clinometer. Back in the lab they plot these observations on a base map. Connecting similar observations reveals underground patterns. This shows how rock formations extend beneath areas with no outcrops.
